title: Isolation of Monocytes from Whole Blood With Magnetic Negative Sorting
author: Brandt D Pence
license_note: Confirmed via CrossRef DOI metadata: dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.kwtcxen

steps.title: Collect blood into K2EDTA vacutainer tube and invert several times
steps.instruction: Collect blood into K2EDTA vacutainer tube and invert several times. Store at RT until use.

steps.title: Aliquot whole blood into 14 ml tubes
steps.instruction: Aliquot whole blood into 14 ml tubes. Each tube receives 4 ml of blood. A successful isolation will yield 1-2×10^6 total cells (>90% monocytes) per 4 ml of blood.

steps.title: Vortex RapidSpheres (brown reagent) from monocyte...
steps.instruction: Vortex RapidSpheres (brown reagent) from monocyte isolation kit well (15-30 sec). Vortex isolation cocktail (clear reagent) from monocyte isolation kit (5 sec).

steps.title: Add 200 μl RapidSpheres and 200...
steps.instruction: Add 200 μl RapidSpheres and 200 μl isolation cocktail to each 14 ml tube. Pulse vortex tubes 5-10 times to mix. Incubate 5 min at room temperature.

steps.title: Add 8 ml isolation buffer to...
steps.instruction: Add 8 ml isolation buffer to each tube and invert 5 times to mix (make sure to hold tube cap down tightly to avoid spilling sample). Move tubes to magnet and incubate 5 min at room temperature.

steps.title: Remove supernatant from tubes by pipetting...
steps.instruction: Remove supernatant from tubes by pipetting and transfer to new 14 ml tube. Supernatants will be a somewhat clarified red color. Red blood cells will not stick well to the magnet during the first transfer step (this will not affect performance). For best yield, transfer ~ 0.5 ml of red blood cells from as well.

steps.title: Add 200 μl RapidSpheres and 200...
steps.instruction: Add 200 μl RapidSpheres and 200 μl isolation cocktail to each 14 ml tube. Invert 5 times to mix (make sure to hold tube cap down tightly to avoid spilling sample). Incubate 5 min at room temperature.

steps.title: Move tubes to magnet and incubate...
steps.instruction: Move tubes to magnet and incubate at least 5 minutes at room temperature. A 10 minute incubation on this step appears to improve red blood cell removal and does not affect cell viability.

steps.title: Remove supernatant from tubes by pipetting...
steps.instruction: Remove supernatant from tubes by pipetting and transfer to new 14 ml tube. Supernatants will now be a clarified yellow color similar to blood plasma. Red blood cells should stick well to the magnet during this transfer step. Do not remove or disturb red blood cells.

steps.title: Move new tubes directly to the...
steps.instruction: Move new tubes directly to the magnet and incubate 5 minutes at room temperature.

steps.title: Remove supernatant from tubes by pipetting...
steps.instruction: Remove supernatant from tubes by pipetting and transfer to appropriate tubes for centrifugation (note 1). Supernatants will be a clarified yellow color similar to blood plasma. Residual red blood cells should form a very thin line on the back of the tube against the magnet. Do not remove or disturb red blood cells. (1) We use 5 ml tubes (3 per 14 ml tube) for this step as we do not have a good way to centrifuge the 14 ml tubes in our lab. Choice of tube will depend on availability of rotors for the centrifuge.

steps.title: Centrifuge cells at 500 × g...
steps.instruction: Centrifuge cells at 500 × g for 5 min in refrigerated centrifuge (4-8degC) to pellet cells.

steps.title: Aspirate supernatants and resuspend in prepared...
steps.instruction: Aspirate supernatants and resuspend in prepared media or buffer for counting (note 2). A volume of 75-100 μl of media per 1 ml of initial blood that was processed seems to work well and consistently gives a final cell concentration of 4-6 × 10^6 cells / ml. (2) If red blood cells are visible in the pellet, a red blood cell lysis step can be inserted here. This is usually not needed and will somewhat decrease total cell yield.

steps.title: Count cells using routine methods (note 3)
steps.instruction: Count cells using routine methods (note 3). Cell viability should be near 100%. (3) We use a Scepter counter with a 40 μm sensor and dilute the cell suspension 10× in media prior to counting (20 μl cells in 180 μl media). Note that the Scepter requires serum-free medium as presence of serum in the media will clog the sensor.

steps.title: Proceed to downstream applications
steps.instruction: Proceed to downstream applications. Cell purity can be checked using flow cytometer with an anti-CD14 antibody (note 4). (4) Note that this particular kit contains anti-CD16 antibodies and thus depletes intermediate (CD14 + CD16 + ) and non-classical monocytes (CD14 dim CD16 ++ ). A monocyte kit without CD16 depletion (#19058) is available from Stem Cell Technologies.

steps.title: Materials to Prepare
steps.instruction: Materials to Prepare: **Isolation Buffer**: 1 mM EDTA in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS): -Add 2 ml 0.5 M EDTA to a 1 L bottle of sterile PBS. -Maintain sterility. -Store at room temperature. -Usable at least 6 months. **Media** -Various media can be used, depending on downstream assay. -For counting using a Scepter cell counter (Millipore), serum-free medium is necessary.
